runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation":"Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for already-working prime-age U.S. workers: percent change in annual hours from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation.","point_estimate":0.4,"quantiles":{"p05":0.1,"p25":0.25,"p50":0.4,"p75":0.7,"p95":1.2},"citations":["Chetty et al. on labor supply elasticities and micro vs macro wedges","MaCurdy on estimation of intertemporal substitution in labor supply","Rogerson and Wallenius / Prescott-style macro calibration discussions"],"reasoning_summary":"My central belief is that the intensive-margin Frisch elasticity for prime-age employed U.S. workers is modest but clearly above zero, with micro evidence often clustering around roughly 0.3–0.6, while some macro calibrations use values closer to or above 1. Excluding participation and focusing on prime-age workers pulls the estimate below extensive-margin or aggregate elasticities."}
